User Guide

Maths Question Modelling Tool

Break a word problem down into maths the way you'd do it on the board — one step at a time: state the goal, name the variables, write the equations, then solve.

Open the tool https://amkamz.github.io/math-problem-breakdown-tool/ Open →

Contents

  1. Presenting a word problem
  2. Creating a word problem
  3. Other controls & extras

The three steps are colour-coded everywhere in the tool:

  • 1 The goal — orange
  • 2 Variables — blue
  • 3 Equations — green

1 Presenting a word problem

This is the student-facing Present view. You reveal the working piece by piece by clicking the words in the problem — the algebra always stays tied to the words that produced it.

  1. Open the tool. Go to https://amkamz.github.io/math-problem-breakdown-tool/ in a web browser. A problem loads automatically, with the problem on the left and the three empty working boxes (Goal, Variables, Equations) on the right.
  2. Open the menu and choose your problem. Click menu ▴ at the bottom-right to show the toolbar, then pick a question from the Problem dropdown.
  3. Choose a step. Click a step button on the left (1 The goal, 2 Variables, 3 Equations) — or click the heading of any box on the right. The chosen step lights up, the other two boxes dim, and a hint appears telling you what to click.
  4. Click the words in the problem. With a step active, click the relevant phrase in the problem text. It highlights in that step's colour and the matching working appears in the box on the right. (For the goal, the blank fills in — first in words, then rewritten into symbols once variables are named.)
  5. Do steps 2 and 3 the same way. Switch to Variables and click each quantity to name it; then switch to Equations and click each fact to turn it into an equation. A tick appears on every step you have filled in.
  6. Reveal the solution, line by line. Once at least one equation is showing, click anywhere in the Equations box (or press Show solution) to reveal the worked solution one line at a time. Keep clicking for the next line until it reads Solution complete.
Tip: Double-click anywhere in the left panel to briefly flash where the current step's words are, if a class gets stuck. Click a highlighted phrase again to hide it, and use Reset in the menu to clear everything and start the problem fresh.

2 Creating a word problem

Switch to the Author view to write your own question. You type the problem, mark which words belong to each step, and write the solution — a Live preview on the right mirrors exactly what students will see.

  1. Start a new problem. Open the menu, click New, and give the question a name. (Or click Author to edit the problem already on screen.) You land in the Author view.
  2. Type the title and the problem text. Fill in the Title and Problem text boxes. Write the problem exactly as students should read it.
  3. Mark the words for each step. In the Problem text box, select a word or phrase, then click ＋ Goal, ＋ Variable, or ＋ Equation to attach it to that step. The same words can belong to more than one step.
  4. Say what each mark reveals. For a Variable or Equation, fill in the box with what should appear — wrap any maths in dollar signs, e.g. Let $t$ = time in seconds. For the Goal, fill in Find… (words) and Find… (symbols), plus the optional when… fields.
  5. Write the solution. Under Solution steps, click ＋ Add solution line for each line of working, wrapping maths in $…$. Use the ↑ and ✕ buttons to reorder or remove lines.
  6. Check it in the Live preview. On the right, pick a step and click the highlighted words — exactly as students will. Everything updates as you type, so you can confirm the goal rewrites correctly and the maths renders.
  7. Save. Click Save in the editing panel. Your problem now appears in the Problem dropdown in Present view.
If you edit the problem text later: a mark whose words were changed shows ⚠ text changed — reselect the words below. Just re-select those words in the text box and click Re-select on that row to re-anchor it.
Saving to real files (desktop Chrome/Edge): click Open folder in the menu to read and write actual .json files in a folder — handy for keeping a question bank under version control. Otherwise problems are saved inside the browser, and you can still Export any problem as a JSON file.

3 Other controls & extras

Everything else lives in the bottom toolbar (click menu to show it).

Present / Author / Framework
Switch views. Framework is a one-page explainer of the three-step method to show students.
Problem ▾  ·  New
Choose which question to present, or start a new one.
A−   A+
Make all the text smaller or larger — useful for projecting to a class.
Reset
Clear every reveal and start the current problem fresh.
Worksheet (blank) / Answer key  ·  Print
Choose a mode, then Print to produce a printable A4 sheet: a blank worksheet for students, or the full answer key.
Export / Import
Export saves the current problem as a JSON file; Import loads problems from a JSON file.
Open folder
Desktop Chrome/Edge only — read and write loose .json problem files in a folder you choose.
On an iPad or phone: open the link in Safari, then Share → Add to Home Screen for a full-screen, app-like icon. Presenting works everywhere; authoring to loose files is desktop-only.
First-load note: the maths and fonts load from the internet the first time, then cache. The first open needs a connection; after that it works offline.
